section.title: Hear a Just Cause
section.sectionRationale: An individual lament in the form of a legal plea, David appealing to God as righteous judge to examine his heart, vindicate his innocence against violent enemies, and keep him as the apple of God's eye. It closes with one of the Psalter's earliest hints of hope beyond death, awaking satisfied in God's presence.
section.thesis: David appeals boldly to God's own examination of his heart, asking to be kept as the apple of God's eye and hidden under His wings, and closes by anticipating a satisfaction found only in beholding God's face.
section.frameNote: Four movements: David's plea for a hearing of his righteous cause; his declaration of innocence tested by God Himself; his urgent request for protection as the apple of God's eye from violent enemies; and his closing hope of awaking satisfied in God's presence.

units.label: Hear my righteous plea
units.note: an appeal for a just hearing

verses.text: Hear, O LORD, my righteous plea; listen to my cry. Give ear to my prayer, it comes from lips free of deceit.
May my vindication come from Your presence; may Your eyes see what is right.

units.label: You have tested me and found no evil

verses.text: You have tried my heart; You have visited me in the night. You have tested me and found no evil; I have resolved not to sin with my mouth.

As for the deeds of men, by the word of Your lips 
I have avoided the ways of the violent.
My steps have held to Your paths; my feet have not slipped.

units.label: Keep me as the apple of Your eye

verses.text: I call on You, O God, for You will answer me. Incline Your ear to me; hear my words.

Show the wonders of Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}, You who save by Your right hand 
those who seek refuge from their foes.
Keep me as the {{ishon|apple of Your eye}}; hide me in the shadow of Your wings
from the wicked who assail me, from my mortal enemies who surround me.
They have closed their callous hearts; their mouths speak with arrogance.
They have tracked us down, and now surround us; their eyes are set to cast us to the ground,
like a lion greedy for prey, like a young lion lurking in ambush.
Arise, O LORD, confront them! Bring them to their knees; deliver me from the wicked by Your sword,

from such men, O LORD, by Your hand, from men of the world whose portion is in this life. May You fill the bellies of Your treasured ones and satisfy their sons, so they leave their abundance to their children.
As for me, I will behold Your face in righteousness; when I awake, I will be satisfied in Your presence.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant loving devotion. David asks God to show the wonders of this loving devotion specifically to those who take refuge in Him, grounding his plea not in his own merit but in God's covenant character.

ishon.gloss: Literally 'little man,' the pupil of the eye, the small reflection one sees of oneself in another's eye, used idiomatically for something treasured and carefully guarded. David asks to be kept as precious and protected as the pupil of God's own eye.

basic.context: David appeals to God as judge, asking Him to hear a righteous cause spoken without deceit. He declares that God Himself has already tested his heart at night and found no evil in him regarding the specific charges his enemies bring. He then urgently asks to be kept as the apple of God's eye and hidden under His wings from violent enemies who stalk him like lions, appealing for God to arise and deliver him. The psalm closes with a quiet but striking hope: David expects to awake and be satisfied, beholding God's face in righteousness.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not a claim to sinless perfection but a legal plea of innocence regarding specific false accusations and violent pursuit; David appeals to God's own searching examination as his defense. Its closing verse offers an early, undeveloped hint of hope beyond death that later revelation, especially the resurrection of Christ, brings into full light.
basic.originalLanguages: David asks God to show the wonders of His chesed, covenant loving devotion, to those who seek refuge in Him, and pleads to be kept as ishon, the apple or pupil of God's eye, a vivid image of being treasured and closely guarded, hidden further still in the shadow of God's wings.
basic.typology: David's plea to be vindicated by God's own examination and kept as the apple of His eye anticipates the perfect innocence of Christ, who alone could truly say His accusers found no fault in Him, and who was vindicated by God through the resurrection.
basic.christ: David's confidence of a righteous vindication before God and his hope of beholding God's face anticipate the greater righteousness of Christ, the only truly innocent sufferer, whose resurrection and ascension are the ultimate vindication this psalm reaches toward, and through whom believers now have the hope of one day beholding God's face, Revelation 22:4.
basic.god: God is shown as a righteous judge who tests hearts honestly, as a refuge whose loving devotion protects those who flee to Him, and as the one whose presence alone finally satisfies, a satisfaction awaiting even beyond death.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 32:10 uses the same image of the apple of the eye for God's care of Israel. 1 Peter 2:22-23 shows Christ as the truly innocent sufferer who entrusted Himself to the righteous judge. Revelation 22:4 promises believers will see God's face.
basic.oneStory: David's plea to be examined, vindicated, and kept as precious as the apple of God's eye reaches toward a satisfaction found only in beholding God's face, a hope the whole Bible shows is secured for all who trust in Christ, the truly innocent one whom God has vindicated and exalted.

context.literary: An individual lament shaped as a legal plea, moving from an appeal for a righteous hearing, through a declaration of tested innocence, to an urgent cry for protection using vivid imagery of the apple of the eye and the shadow of wings, and closing with a brief but significant statement of hope beyond death.
context.historical: Likely composed during one of the periods when David was violently pursued by enemies who accused him falsely, possibly during Saul's pursuit or Absalom's rebellion, though the psalm's language is general enough to be prayed in any similar circumstance of unjust persecution.
context.canonical: Stands among several psalms of innocence appealing to God as righteous judge, and its closing hope of awaking satisfied in God's presence is one of the Psalter's earliest, though undeveloped, hints of hope beyond physical death.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To plead David's innocence regarding specific false accusations and violent persecution, appealing directly to God's own righteous examination rather than to David's self-assessment, and to express confident hope in God's ultimate vindication and presence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific legal plea regarding particular circumstances; it prescribes, more broadly, the pattern of bringing accusations of injustice honestly before God as the righteous judge rather than taking private vengeance.

debates.question: Does verse 15's hope of 'awaking satisfied' refer to a full doctrine of bodily resurrection or something more limited?

views.label: It expresses a genuine, if not fully developed, hope of resurrection and future vision of God, an early seed that later revelation brings to full clarity
views.case: The language of awaking and beholding God's face closely parallels later resurrection hope in Daniel 12:2 and is read by much of the Christian tradition as anticipating bodily resurrection, though David himself likely had limited clarity about how this would occur.

views.label: It primarily expresses hope of waking each morning to renewed fellowship with God after a night of trial, a this life confidence rather than an explicit resurrection claim
views.case: The immediate context concerns deliverance from present enemies rather than death itself, and 'awake' may simply picture the morning after a night of prayer and testing, as in verse 3's reference to God visiting him in the night.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the verse expresses genuine confidence in God's presence as ultimately satisfying; Christian reading, informed by the fuller resurrection hope revealed in Christ, rightly hears in this verse an early note of the hope fully realized in the gospel.

study.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's chesed, covenant loving devotion, as the ground of his rescue, and asks to be kept as ishon, the apple of God's eye, a picture of being treasured and carefully watched over, further pictured as hidden in the shadow of God's protecting wings.

typology.type: David's plea of tested innocence before violent, false accusers, verses 3-5
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the only truly innocent sufferer, of whom Peter writes that when He was reviled, He did not revile in return, but entrusted Himself to Him who judges justly, 1 Peter 2:23.

typology.type: David's hope of awaking satisfied, beholding God's face, verse 15
typology.fulfillment: The full resurrection hope and promised sight of God secured for believers through Christ, Revelation 22:4, 1 John 3:2.

study.christ: David's plea to be vindicated by God's own examination and his confidence of ultimately beholding God's face anticipate the pattern fulfilled perfectly in Christ, the truly innocent sufferer who entrusted Himself to the Father's righteous judgment and was vindicated through resurrection. Through union with Christ, believers now share David's hope of one day beholding God's face and being fully satisfied in His presence.
study.god: God is the righteous judge who genuinely tests the heart rather than merely accepting claims of innocence, the refuge whose loving devotion protects those who flee to Him from violent enemies, and the One whose presence alone finally and fully satisfies.

crossReferences.note: Uses the same image of the apple of the eye to describe God's tender, careful protection of Israel in the wilderness.
Describes Christ as the truly innocent sufferer who, unlike ordinary sinners, entrusted Himself entirely to the righteous judge.
Promises that believers will see God's face, the full and final answer to David's hope in verse 15.

study.oneStory: David pleads his innocence before God, asks to be kept as precious as the apple of His eye, and closes with a hope of awaking satisfied in God's presence. That hope finds its full ground in Christ, the truly innocent one vindicated by resurrection, through whom all who trust Him will one day behold God's face and be satisfied forever.

quiz.q: What kind of prayer is Psalm 17, in its overall form?

options.text: A legal plea of innocence, appealing to God as righteous judge against violent, false accusers.
options.note: The psalm's language of vindication, testing, and a righteous hearing marks it as a legal appeal.

options.text: A hymn of pure celebration with no element of complaint.
options.note: The psalm contains urgent complaint and plea against enemies, not pure celebration.

options.text: A wisdom psalm offering general life advice.
options.note: While it has some reflective elements, its dominant form is a legal lament and plea, not general wisdom instruction.

options.text: A psalm of corporate national lament over exile.
options.note: The psalm is personal and individual in its complaint, not focused on national exile.

quiz.q: What does David claim in verses 3-5 regarding God's testing of him?

options.text: That God Himself tested his heart at night and found no evil regarding the specific accusations against him.
options.note: David appeals to God's own examination as evidence of his innocence in this specific matter.

options.text: That he has never sinned in any way throughout his entire life.
options.note: This overstates the claim; David pleads specific innocence regarding these accusations, not sinless perfection generally.

options.text: That his enemies have also confirmed his innocence.
options.note: It is God's testing, not the enemies' confirmation, that David appeals to as evidence.

options.text: That he no longer needs to pray for God's guidance.
options.note: David continues to appeal urgently to God throughout the psalm, showing ongoing dependence on prayer.

quiz.q: What does David ask for in verse 8, and what does the image mean?

options.text: To be kept as the apple of God's eye, ishon, a picture of being treasured and carefully guarded.
options.note: This vivid idiom pictures something as precious and closely watched as the pupil of one's own eye.

options.text: To receive literal physical healing for an eye ailment.
options.note: The phrase is a figurative idiom for being treasured, not a literal medical request.

options.text: To gain greater physical eyesight for battle.
options.note: The request is about being protected and treasured by God, not about physical vision.

options.text: To be given prophetic visions of the future.
options.note: This misunderstands the idiom, which concerns being precious and protected, not receiving visions.

quiz.q: What hope does David express in the psalm's closing verse, verse 15?

options.text: That he will behold God's face in righteousness and awake satisfied in His presence.
options.note: This closing hope reaches beyond David's immediate circumstances toward lasting satisfaction in God's presence.

options.text: That his enemies will convert and become his friends.
options.note: The psalm's closing hope concerns David's own vindication and satisfaction in God, not his enemies' conversion.

options.text: That he will receive great material wealth in this life.
options.note: Verse 14 contrasts David's hope with 'men of the world whose portion is in this life,' distinguishing his hope from material gain.

options.text: That his descendants alone will benefit from God's blessing.
options.note: While verse 14 mentions descendants of the wicked, David's own personal hope in verse 15 is about beholding God's face himself.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's closing hope connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates the fuller hope of resurrection and beholding God's face secured for believers through Christ, as in Revelation 22:4.
options.note: David's early, undeveloped hint of hope beyond death finds its full clarity and fulfillment in the resurrection secured by Christ.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to any New Testament teaching.
options.note: Its language of awaking and beholding God's face closely parallels New Testament resurrection and beatific vision themes.

options.text: It predicts the specific date of Christ's second coming.
options.note: The psalm expresses a hope of presence with God, not a specific predicted date for any future event.

options.text: It refers only to David's hope for a peaceful night's sleep with no further significance.
options.note: This understates the verse's deeper hope, widely read as anticipating a satisfaction beyond ordinary rest.

questions: David appeals to God's own examination of his heart rather than defending himself in his own words. Where do you need to bring an accusation or misunderstanding honestly to God rather than only trying to defend yourself?

David asks to be kept as the apple of God's eye and hidden under His wings. What would it look like this week to actually rest in that kind of treasured protection rather than trying to protect yourself?

David distinguishes his hope from 'men of the world whose portion is in this life' who look only for present abundance. Where might you be tempted to measure your life's success only by what you can gain or enjoy now?

David's closing hope is simply to behold God's face and be satisfied. What competing sources of satisfaction, achievement, comfort, approval, are you tempted to settle for instead of that deeper satisfaction found only in God's presence?

Christ, the truly innocent sufferer, entrusted Himself to the Father rather than defending Himself before His accusers. How does His example shape the way you respond when you are wrongly accused or mistreated?

translationNotes.note: Give ear to my prayer, it comes from lips free of deceit renders a line that in the source BSB text used a dash before 'it comes'; here it is rendered with a comma to preserve the sense without using a dash.
Apple of Your eye renders ishon, literally 'little man,' referring to the pupil, where a small reflection of oneself can be seen, an idiom for something treasured and carefully guarded.
When I awake, I will be satisfied in Your presence is one of the Psalter's earliest hints of hope beyond death, later developed fully in New Testament resurrection teaching.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 17:1-15. The source text contained em dashes in verses 1, 4, and 14, each converted to a comma or clean break per house style; no em or en dashes remain in this file.
